title: Tamil Chicken Kulambu
description: A weekday Tamil chicken curry simmered in a ground masala of shallots, tomato, coconut and warm spices. The gravy is thin enough to soak into rice and bold enough to stand up to dosa the next morning.

steps.text: Grind the coconut with fennel seeds and a splash of water into a smooth paste. Set aside.
Heat gingelly oil in a kadai on medium flame. Splutter mustard seeds, then add curry leaves and the sliced shallots. Saute until the shallots turn soft and golden at the edges, about 5 minutes.
Add ginger-garlic paste and fry until the raw smell goes. Add tomatoes with a pinch of salt and cook until they collapse and the oil starts to separate.
Lower the flame and add chilli powder, coriander powder and turmeric. Stir for 30 seconds — do not let the powders burn.
Add the chicken and turn the flame to high. Sear, turning, until the pieces lose their pink colour and are coated in masala, about 5 minutes.
Pour in 400 ml hot water and salt. Cover and simmer on medium for 15 minutes until the chicken is nearly cooked.
Stir in the coconut-fennel paste. Simmer uncovered for 8–10 minutes until the gravy thickens slightly and oil floats on top.
Check salt, finish with coriander leaves, and rest 10 minutes before serving — the kulambu tastes better as it sits. Serve with rice, idli or dosa.

substitutions.substitute: 3 tbsp coconut milk powder or 100 ml coconut milk
500 g soya chunks (soaked) for a vegetarian kulambu

culturalNote: Kozhi kulambu is the default Sunday curry across Tamil Nadu homes, made loose enough to mix generously with steamed rice.
regionalVariation: Chettinad versions add star anise, stone flower (kalpasi) and black pepper for a darker, hotter gravy; Kongunadu homes often skip tomato and lean on coconut.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Thayir Sadam (Tempered Curd Rice)
description: Cooling South Indian curd rice — soft rice mashed with fresh curd and milk, finished with a crackling mustard-ginger tempering. The dish that ends every Tamil meal and rescues every hot afternoon.

ingredients.name: milk
ingredients.notes: keeps the rice from turning sour in a lunchbox

steps.text: Pressure cook the rice with 2.5 cups water for 4 whistles so it is softer than usual, or use day-old rice mashed with a splash of hot water.
Let the rice cool until just warm. Mash it lightly with the back of a ladle — you want it creamy, not pasty.
Mix in the curd, milk and salt until smooth. The mixture should be loose; it thickens as it sits.
Heat oil in a small tempering pan. Splutter mustard seeds, add urad dal and fry until golden, then add ginger, green chilli, curry leaves and asafoetida. Fry for 30 seconds.
Pour the tempering over the curd rice and fold through. Garnish with pomegranate or grated carrot. Serve at room temperature or lightly chilled, with pickle.

substitutions.substitute: plant-based yogurt for a dairy-free version
cooked millet (thinai/samai) for a lighter version

substitutions.notes: soak 10 minutes first for even frying

culturalNote: No Tamil meal truly ends without thayir sadam — it settles the stomach after spicy courses and is the classic temple prasadam and travel food.
regionalVariation: In Karnataka, mosaranna adds pomegranate and cucumber; Iyengar households temper with a little grated mango ginger when in season.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Turkish Yogurt Chicken with Buttered Rice
description: Chicken marinated in garlicky yogurt, seared until blistered, and served over buttery rice with a paprika-chilli butter spooned on top. A Turkish home classic that happens to be perfect for Indian kitchens — curd, chicken, rice.

steps.text: Whisk half the curd with garlic, lemon juice, half the paprika, pepper and salt. Coat the chicken and marinate 15 minutes on the counter (or up to overnight in the fridge).
Wash the rice and cook it with 2.5 cups water and a little salt — in a pot, rice cooker, or 2 whistles in a pressure cooker. Fluff with 1 tbsp of the butter.
Heat oil in a wide tawa or skillet on high. Lay the chicken strips in a single layer and sear without moving for 3 minutes until charred spots form, then turn and cook 4–5 minutes more until done.
In a small pan, melt the remaining butter with the chopped onion until soft. Add grated tomato and remaining paprika and simmer 3 minutes into a loose, brick-red butter sauce.
Plate rice, top with chicken, spoon over the remaining whisked curd, then drizzle the paprika butter on top. Eat immediately while the butter is still sizzling.

substitutions.substitute: ghee — closer to the traditional taste than oil
paneer (400 g) — skip searing beyond 2 minutes per side
Kashmiri chilli powder gives the colour without much heat

culturalNote: Yogurt-sauced meats over bread or rice are everyday food across Turkey — the paprika butter poured at the table is the signature move.
regionalVariation: The famous İskender kebap version uses sliced bread under the meat and doner instead of pan-seared chicken.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Everything here is standard Indian grocery: curd instead of Turkish yogurt (hang it in a muslin cloth for 15 minutes so it doesn't split), Kashmiri chilli for paprika, and a tawa instead of a cast-iron grill. Ghee can replace butter.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen
